ai-ethernet.definition: Ethernet-based AI networking systems — switches, fabric platforms, SDN control planes — that interconnect accelerators within and across clusters (Arista, Cisco).

architecture-ip.definition: Licensable processor / architecture IP — CPU and GPU cores, instruction sets — supplied to chip designers rather than as finished silicon (ARM, RISC-V).

compute-memory.definition: Functional domain — the silicon and IP that perform computation and hold working data: merchant GPUs / CPUs, custom accelerators, memory and storage, the architecture and memory-interface IP they integrate, and the on-package / in-rack high-speed I/O that links them.

coupe-io.definition: Co-packaged optical I/O engines (e.g. TSMC COUPE) that integrate optical interconnect directly with compute silicon for energy-efficient scale-out bandwidth.

cpo-switch-asic.definition: Co-packaged-optics switch silicon and optical DSPs / retimers that drive optical links — Broadcom Tomahawk photonics, Marvell optical DSP.

custom-asic.definition: Purpose-built AI accelerators and custom silicon designed for a specific buyer or workload — hyperscaler TPU / Trainium-class parts and the design houses (Broadcom, Marvell) that co-develop them. Distinct from stack.gpu-cpu (merchant general-purpose accelerators).

dci.definition: Long-haul and metro optical transport linking data centers — coherent DCI systems and transponders (Ciena-class).

design-manufacturing.definition: Functional domain — the design tools and front-end fabrication that bring a chip into existence: EDA software, advanced-node foundry manufacturing, and the capital equipment for wafer processing and advanced packaging. Enablers of compute rather than compute itself.

eda.definition: Electronic-design-automation tools — synthesis, place-and-route, verification, simulation — used to design every chip (Synopsys, Cadence, Siemens EDA).

fiber.definition: Optical fiber and cabling — the passive physical medium for optical interconnect, intra- and inter-data-center (Corning fiber).

foundry.definition: Front-end wafer fabrication at advanced process nodes — TSMC, Samsung Foundry, Intel Foundry. Where compute silicon is physically manufactured.

gpu-cpu.definition: General-purpose merchant AI compute silicon — data-center GPUs and host CPUs sold to any buyer (NVIDIA, AMD). Distinct from stack.custom-asic (accelerators purpose-built for a single buyer or workload).

high-speed-connectivity.definition: Connectivity-chip silicon at the scale-out / cluster level — retimers, active-cable, and fabric-link silicon connecting accelerators across nodes and racks (Credo, MACOM, Semtech, Astera Labs). The cluster-scale counterpart to stack.high-speed-io (the in-node / on-package view); an actor spanning both carries both positions.

high-speed-io.definition: On-package and in-rack data-movement silicon — SerDes, PCIe / CXL retimers and switches, CXL memory controllers — that links compute, memory, and peripherals at high bandwidth WITHIN a node or rack (Astera Labs, Credo). The in-rack view of connectivity silicon; its scale-out / cluster-level counterpart is stack.high-speed-connectivity (Interconnect & Optical). An actor whose products span both views carries both positions.

interconnect-optical.definition: Functional domain — the optical and network technology that connects accelerators across nodes and data centers: silicon photonics and co-packaged optics, optical modules and DSPs, scale-out connectivity silicon, AI Ethernet and DCI systems, optical EMS, and fiber.

liquid-cooling.definition: Thermal management for dense AI racks — cold plates, CDUs, immersion, heat-rejection — required once per-rack power exceeds air-cooling limits.

memory-interface-ip.definition: Licensable controller / PHY IP for memory interfaces (DDR / LPDDR / HBM) integrated into SoCs. The interface-IP tier feeding memory connectivity; distinct from stack.memory-storage (the memory devices themselves).

memory-storage.definition: Semiconductor memory and storage attached to compute — DRAM, HBM, NAND, enterprise SSD / HDD. The capacity-and-bandwidth tier feeding the accelerators.

optical-ems.definition: Precision contract manufacturing / EMS for optical and photonic assemblies — the build-side of the optical-module supply chain.

optical-modules.definition: Pluggable optical transceiver modules and their components (lasers, photodetectors), assembled from photonics and electronics — Coherent, Lumentum, InnoLight, AOI.

packaging-equipment.definition: Capital equipment for advanced packaging and 3D integration — hybrid bonders, thermal-compression bonders, back-end lithography / deposition — supplied to foundries and OSATs. Grouped with Design & Manufacturing as front-end / capital tooling, distinct from the back-end packaging SERVICE (stack.packaging-test).

packaging-substrate.definition: Functional domain — the back-end assembly and carriers that turn finished dies into deployable modules: advanced packaging and final test (CoWoS, fan-out, 3D stacking) plus the IC substrates and high-layer-count PCBs that carry the die to the board.

packaging-test.definition: Back-end assembly, advanced packaging (CoWoS, fan-out, 3D stacking), and final test — TSMC in-house packaging plus OSATs (ASE, Amkor). The throughput bottleneck for AI-accelerator output.

power-semi.definition: Power-conversion semiconductors and modules (SiC / GaN, VRMs, power stages) that deliver and regulate current to accelerators at rack and board level — Vicor, Power Integrations, Navitas.

power-thermal.definition: Functional domain — the power delivery and heat removal that keep dense AI racks operating: power-conversion semiconductors and modules (SiC / GaN, VRMs, power stages) and liquid-cooling systems (cold plates, CDUs, immersion). Neither is packaging; both gate deployable rack density.

siph-foundry.definition: Photonics-capable foundry processes (GlobalFoundries Fotonix, TSMC SiPh, Intel SiPh) that fabricate silicon-photonics ICs — the SiPh analogue of stack.foundry. Grouped under Interconnect & Optical (not Design & Manufacturing) because its output is exclusively optical interconnect silicon.

substrate-pcb.definition: IC package substrates (ABF organic, emerging glass-core) and high-layer-count PCBs that carry the die to the board — Ibiden, Unimicron, Shinko.

theme.ai-financing.definition: The capital-structure narrative of the AI buildout — vendor financing, GPU-backed debt, special-purpose vehicles, and circular flows where a supplier funds its own customers (the Burry thesis). An analyst / short narrative, not a tradable basket (no ETF), but graph-native: the project already models VCI, Apollo debt financing, and vendor-financing loops. Membership = an actor materially exposed to or structuring AI vendor / SPV financing.

theme.ai-power.definition: Electricity generation, grid, and on-site power as the binding constraint on AI data-center buildout — utilities / IPPs, gas turbines, transformers and switchgear, nuclear / SMR, and the facility-side power & thermal (liquid cooling) hardware that gates deployable rack density. The 2026 consensus-bottleneck theme; ETF: AIPO. Folds the nuclear / SMR and liquid-cooling sub-narratives.

theme.photonics.definition: Light replaces copper as the high-bandwidth interconnect backbone of AI data centers — silicon photonics, co-packaged optics, optical transceivers / modules and DSPs, the lasers and III-V materials that feed them, photonics-capable foundries, and optical EMS. Cross-cutting: spans materials makers (AXT, IQE), foundries (GlobalFoundries, X-FAB), laser / component vendors (Coherent, Lumentum, Sivers), and networking / ASIC players (Broadcom, Marvell). Dedicated ETFs: FOTO, LYTE, EUV. Membership = a defensible role in moving data with light, or supplying that stack (THM-004).

theme.physical-ai.definition: Embodied AI — humanoid robots, actuators, sensors, and the edge compute that runs them — as the next demand wave for AI silicon and an application surface beyond the data center. Record VC funding through 2026; analyst frameworks (Barclays two-wave).

theme.semicap.definition: The capital-equipment vendors that enable all AI silicon regardless of who wins at the chip level — lithography, deposition, etch, metrology, epitaxy, and test (ASML, Applied Materials, Lam Research, KLA, Teradyne, Advantest, Aixtron). The mature picks-and-shovels basket; ETFs: SMH, SOXX.
